hypotheses: Hypotheses involve examining the moderation of the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE) by socioeconomic status (SES) and academic self-regulation constructs.
Intrinsic motivation, self-efficacy, and elaboration are hypothesized to positively moderate BFLPE.
Memorization and anxiety are hypothesized to negatively moderate BFLPE.
Sense of belonging to school is anticipated to positively moderate BFLPE.
claim.hypotheses_location: Section describing Hypotheses and Research Questions
claim.statement: The study examines the Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect (BFLPE), proposing that students in high-ability schools often have lower academic self-concepts compared to their peers in lower-ability settings.

data.source: 2003 Program for International Student Assessment (PISA) database
data.notes: Data derives from a hierarchical structure (students nested within schools and countries).

method.description: Multilevel modeling utilized on PISA data to evaluate BFLPE's generalizability across student characteristics.
steps: Obtain data from the 2003 PISA survey.
Assess BFLPE impact through multilevel regression analysis accounting for hierarchical structure.
Evaluate SES and other constructs as potential moderators.

results.summary: The BFLPE was confirmed, with high-ability school attendance linked to lower academic self-concept.

metadata.original_paper_title: The Big-Fish-Little-Pond Effect: Generalizability and Modulation Across Student Characteristics
